common_name: Goldsturm Black-Eyed Susan (Perennial Plant of the Year Rudbeckia)

planting.note: Plant container plants or divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean soil in full sun; Goldsturm is more uniform in habit and flower size than seed-grown Rudbeckia — purchase vegetatively propagated plants or divisions from established clumps for consistent Goldsturm characteristics.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean soil in full sun; Goldsturm is the Perennial Plant Association's 1999 Plant of the Year — a cultivar of the native orange coneflower (Rudbeckia fulgida) that produces larger, more uniform, and more profuse flowers than the species, on a more compact, tidy plant with better habit control than seed-grown wild types. The 3-inch, golden-yellow, daisy-form flowers with a prominent dark-brown central cone bloom from July through September (zone 7–8) in impressive profusion — a well-established Goldsturm clump at peak bloom can produce dozens of simultaneous flowers that create a brilliant golden-yellow mass effect. The long bloom season (8–10 weeks), drought tolerance, adaptability to clay and average soil, and the persistence of the attractive dark seed cones through fall and winter for wildlife habitat make Goldsturm one of the most versatile and reliably satisfying perennials in American horticulture. The dried seed heads attract goldfinches, chickadees, and other seed-eating birds through fall and winter — leaving the heads standing through winter provides both wildlife value and ornamental winter interest. As a native North American perennial, Goldsturm supports numerous native bee species and monarch butterflies throughout the bloom season. In zone 9, Goldsturm blooms through the summer heat more reliably than many perennials, performing at its peak in July through September when the heat is most intense.

common_name: Prairie Sun Rudbeckia (Large Golden Green-Eyed Black-Eyed Susan)

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Prairie Sun is an All-America Selections gold medal winner — one of the most vigorous and long-blooming large-flowered Rudbeckia hirta cultivars available.

directions: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Prairie Sun is one of the finest large-flowered Rudbeckia hirta cultivars — producing very large (4–5 inch diameter), pure golden-yellow flowers with a distinctive apple-green central disc rather than the dark-brown or black center of most black-eyed Susan cultivars. The unusual lime-green center creates a fundamentally different flower appearance — Prairie Sun is technically a 'green-eyed Susan' rather than black-eyed — with the green disc creating a fresh, unconventional combination that is immediately distinctive in the garden. The very large flower size (4–5 inches compared to the 2–3 inches of standard types) and the green center make Prairie Sun outstanding in cut flower arrangements and mass plantings where the large, bold flowers create maximum visual impact. The pollenless flowers (a practical advantage for cut flower use) and exceptional heat and drought tolerance make Prairie Sun outstanding for Gulf South summer gardens. An All-America Selections gold medal winner recognized for its exceptional landscape performance. Plants bloom prolifically from summer through fall without deadheading — though removing spent flowers extends the season. In zone 9, Prairie Sun is typically grown as a summer annual, though it may self-seed and persist in favorable conditions.

common_name: Powis Castle Artemisia (Feathery Silver Shrubby Wormwood)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained to dry, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; Powis Castle requires excellent drainage — it declines rapidly in heavy, wet clay or consistently moist soil, particularly during hot, humid Gulf South summers.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; Powis Castle is the finest and most widely grown ornamental artemisia for mild-climate gardens — a hybrid of uncertain parentage developed at Powis Castle in Wales that produces exceptionally fine, feathery, soft-textured, silver-gray to silver-white foliage on a mounding, somewhat woody plant reaching 2–3 feet tall and 3–5 feet wide. The very fine, finely divided, aromatic foliage creates an outstanding silver-gray texture mass that serves as one of the best companion plants available for brightening and contrasting with adjacent colorful flowers — the silver foliage makes blues bluer, reds redder, and purples more vivid by providing neutral, light-reflective contrast. Unlike some artemisias (like 'Silver King' or 'Silver Queen') that spread aggressively by rhizomes, Powis Castle is clump-forming and relatively non-invasive in garden beds. The plant rarely flowers (a practical advantage since artemisia flowers are inconspicuous and the plant is grown exclusively for foliage) and maintains its fine-textured foliage appearance all season. Sharp drainage is the critical requirement — in the Gulf South's humid summer climate, Powis Castle must be sited in raised beds, berms, or areas with exceptional drainage to prevent root rot during wet periods. With proper siting, it is a remarkably effective and long-lived silver accent plant for full-sun zone 9 borders.

common_name: Florida Sweetheart Caladium (Compact Pink Strap-Leaf Caladium)

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost date when soil has warmed to at least 65°F; plant 1–2 inches deep in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial shade; Florida Sweetheart is a strap-leaf type with higher sun tolerance than standard fancy-leaved types.

directions: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial shade to partial sun; Florida Sweetheart is a University of Florida strap-leaf caladium introduction — a compact variety producing narrower, more elongated, lance-shaped leaves (strap-leaf habit) in rose-pink to medium pink coloration with green margins, creating a soft, delicate pink effect quite different from the bold, large-patterned fancy-leaved types. The smaller leaf size and narrower shape of strap-leaf caladiums create a finer, more graceful texture in mass plantings — particularly effective in edging applications, container gardens, and detailed plantings where the standard 18–24 inch fancy types would be too large in scale. Florida Sweetheart's soft pink coloration creates a romantic, cottage-garden effect in shade positions — the pink tones are effective paired with white companions (white impatiens, white caladiums like 'Candidum', white-flowered ajuga) or with contrasting deep purple foliage (dark heucheras, black ajuga). The higher sun tolerance of strap-leaf caladiums compared to standard fancy types allows Florida Sweetheart to be planted in positions receiving 3–4 hours of morning sun — expanding its usefulness to partially shaded locations that might bleach or damage large white-leaved types.

common_name: Pink Symphony Caladium (All-Pink Large Fancy-Leaved Caladium)

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost date when soil has warmed to at least 65°F; plant 1–2 inches deep in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Pink Symphony requires consistent moisture — irregular watering causes leaf tip burn that reduces the ornamental quality of the display.

directions: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Pink Symphony is a large-leaved, primarily pink-toned fancy caladium producing heart-shaped leaves with varying shades of rose-pink to medium pink as the dominant leaf color, with pink-veined or green-veined patterning depending on individual leaf development. The soft, warm pink coloration creates an elegant, romantic atmosphere in shade garden plantings — distinctly different from the cool white-and-green of 'Candidum' or the vivid red-and-green of 'Florida Cardinal'. Pink caladiums like Pink Symphony are among the most frequently requested by gardeners seeking warm-season color in shaded foundation beds and areas under trees where traditional flowers fail. In zone 9, Pink Symphony performs reliably through the entire warm season (May–October) and the tubers increase in size each year, producing larger and more numerous leaves as the planting matures over successive seasons. The soft pink foliage combines beautifully with white-flowered shade companions (impatiens, begonias, white vinca), silver-leaved plants, and the blue-green tones of hostas for a classic, romantic shade garden color scheme. Maintain consistent moisture throughout the growing season and mulch heavily to maintain soil temperature and moisture.
